ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for braised duck; closest match is the general duck food product class. Braising preparation and soy sauce seasoning are not captured in the ontology code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LQ covers cooked duck meat generically; the specific braising method with soy sauce and spices is not distinguished at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for braised duck as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised duck in Singapore is approximately 150g, representing one bone-in portion or two to three slices of boneless duck meat served over rice.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Duck — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Braised Duck (F11040001). All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry. Saturated fat and sugar values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked duck meat (NDB No. 05140) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised duck in the University of Sydney GI Database. Braised duck contains only 0.6g carbohydrate per 100g, making the glycaemic impact negligible. A GI of approximately 10 is assigned by analogy with other low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes (e.g., soy-braised pork, GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (10 × 0.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.09, rounded to 0.1. This is effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342mg per 100g with minimal potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit braised duck to small portions (≤100g) and request reduced soy sauce preparation, as the 342mg sodium per 100g combined with negligible dietary fibre and low potassium (~180mg/100g) provides insufficient offset to meet a heart-healthy sodium profile; removing the skin further reduces saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.6g/100g) with high protein (19.6g/100g) and negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised duck is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (0.6g/100g) and high protein (19.6g/100g), resulting in a negligible glycaemic load; however, the high total fat (19g/100g) and sodium (342mg/100g) warrant portion control and skin removal to manage cardiovascular risk factors commonly co-occurring with diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume braised duck in moderation, removing the skin to reduce saturated fat from approximately 5.5g to under 3g per 100g, and should request low-sodium soy sauce preparation to bring sodium below 200mg per 100g, as the combination of saturated fat, sodium, and cholesterol at standard preparation levels poses a moderate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.5g/100g) and sodium (342mg/100g) promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Braised duck is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis at standard preparation due to saturated fat (~5.5g/100g) which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and high sodium (342mg/100g) which may worsen tissue swelling; removing the skin and requesting reduced soy sauce are the minimum modifications required.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised duck (卤鸭, Lor Ark) is a classic Singaporean and Southern Chinese dish in which whole duck or duck portions are slow-cooked in a fragrant master stock composed of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, garlic, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, and sugar. The braising process renders the duck meat tender and deeply flavoured, with the skin absorbing the rich, savoury-sweet sauce. The dish is a staple of Teochew and Hokkien culinary traditions and is widely available in Singapore hawker centres, often served sliced over steamed rice or rice porridge with braising liquid as a sauce. Nutritionally, braised duck is energy-dense at 253 kcal per 100g, driven by its high fat content (19g/100g, of which approximately 5.5g is saturated fat). Protein is substantial at 19.6g per 100g, making it a high-protein dish. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.6g per 100g, resulting in a very low glycaemic impact. Sodium is elevated at 342mg per 100g due to soy sauce, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy as a declared allergen. Removing the skin before eating significantly reduces fat and caloric density. The braising spices (ginger, star anise, cinnamon) contribute minor antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties but do not offset the pro-inflammatory effect of the saturated fat at standard preparation levels.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised duck?
Is braised duck high in sodium?
Is braised duck suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11040001 for Braised Duck, which is a canonical primary regional source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sugar, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked duck data (NDB 05140) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; this reduces confidence for those specific fields to medium but the overall record confidence remains high given the primary source anchor.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, entry F11040001, Braised Duck. 2. Saturated fat (5.5g/100g), monounsaturated fat (8.2g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (2.5g/100g), trans fat (0.1g/100g), sugar (0.5g/100g), potassium (180mg/100g), and cholesterol (89mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked duck meat (NDB 05140, Duck, domesticated, meat only, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this entry. These values are approximate and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the braised preparation. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral fractions beyond calcium and iron are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish, and the braised preparation with soy sauce and spices introduces sufficient compositional variability that direct substitution from USDA raw duck values would be unreliable without further validation. 4. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0g/100g per HPB source; this is a confirmed zero from the primary source, consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content of the duck meat itself (soy sauce and spices contribute minimal fibre). 5. The input record incorrectly included 'plant_based' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as duck is an animal product. 6. GI is assigned by analogous estimate (GI ~10) as no direct measurement exists for braised duck; see gi_source for full rationale. 7. Halal status is marked false as standard braised duck preparations in Singapore may use non-halal soy sauce or be prepared in non-halal kitchens; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed for the generic record.
